items.headline: Auto dealership proposed for El Camino Real commercial site
items.summary: Planning Commission will consider a special development permit to allow auto sales at 975 W. El Camino Real. The project involves no building expansion, only site improvements like repaving, restriping, and landscaping upgrades.

items.headline: 195-unit transit-oriented apartment complex permitted near Berryessa
items.summary: A $32 million multi-family development received permits for 195 new apartments at 825 Berryessa Station Way, part of the Berryessa Transit-Oriented Development project. This represents significant housing growth in a transit-accessible location near VTA rail service.

items.headline: Financial Assistance Pilot Program Launches for Water Customers
items.summary: The City Council approved a new financial assistance pilot program to help San José water customers. The program aims to support residents with water bill expenses, though the specific eligibility requirements and funding amount were not detailed in the agenda.

items.headline: City faces potential litigation over Cuesta Park water main incident
items.summary: Mountain View is in closed session discussions with legal counsel regarding potential litigation arising from a water main incident at Cuesta Park. The city is also negotiating real property matters related to parcels at 485 and 495 Clyde Avenue.

items.headline: Planning Commission considers 27-unit townhome project with affordable housing
items.summary: The Planning Commission reviewed a residential development proposal for 27 three-story townhomes, including 5 affordable units. This project represents a significant residential addition to Cupertino's housing supply during an ongoing housing shortage in the Bay Area.

items.headline: Lawsuit filed against city by Mark Fantozzi involving SummerHill Homes
items.summary: A litigation case (Mark Fantozzi v. City of Cupertino, SummerHill Homes, LLC) is proceeding, with SummerHill Homes named as a real party in interest. Details of the dispute were discussed in closed session on May 11.

items.headline: Council to hear 72-unit affordable housing project on Lytton Avenue
items.summary: Palo Alto City Council will hold a public hearing on a six-story, 100% affordable housing development proposed for 450 Lytton Avenue, which would replace a city-owned 52-space parking lot. The 72-unit project is seeking density bonus concessions under state law.

items.headline: City Council adopts policy restricting use of city property for federal civil enforcement
items.summary: Campbell City Council adopted a resolution prohibiting city-owned and city-operated property from being used for federal civil enforcement activities. This policy decision limits how federal agencies can access or utilize municipal facilities and resources.
